schools.summary: Who may eat the holy thing: 'there shall no stranger eat of the holy thing: a sojourner of the priest, or an hired servant, shall not eat of the holy thing' — the restriction — 'There shall no stranger eat of the holy thing': only the priest and his household (those who belonged to him) might eat the holy things, the stranger (one not of the priestly family), the sojourner (a temporary guest), and the hired servant (the day-laborer, not part of the household) excluded (the witnesses noting the significance — the holy things reserved for the priest and those who truly belonged to his house, not the stranger, the guest, or the hired hand, who had no abiding part, the lesson — the figure of who may partake of the holy things of God, not the stranger to the covenant, not the temporary or hireling, but those who truly belong to God's household, 'now therefore ye are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellowcitizens with the saints, and of the household of God,' Ephesians 2:19, the distinction between the hireling and the true member of the house, the hired servant who serves for wages but does not belong, against the son and the bought servant who are of the house, vv.11, the figure — the privilege of the holy things belonging to those who are truly of God's household, the stranger and the hireling excluded, a figure that only those born or bought into God's family partake of the holy things, the spiritual food of the household of faith); the stranger (one not of the priestly family) excluded from the holy things — the restriction (the holy things for the priest's house); the sojourner (the guest) and the hired servant (the hireling, not of the house) excluded — the figure (only those of the household partake, Ephesians 2:19, the providence, the holy things for the priest's house, the bought servant included, vv.11) — Calvin, Keil, Henry, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The rejection of the blemished offering: 'but whatsoever hath a blemish, that shall ye not offer: for it shall not be acceptable for you' — the rejection — 'whatsoever hath a blemish, that shall ye not offer: for it shall not be acceptable for you': no blemished animal might be offered, for it would not be accepted (the witnesses noting the significance — the blemished offering rejected, only the perfect accepted, God will not be served with the maimed and corrupt, but only with the best and the whole, the lesson — the perfection God requires in the offering, the offering must be without blemish to be acceptable, a type of the perfect offering of Christ, the rebuke of those who offer God the lame and the sick, 'ye said also, Behold, what a weariness is it!... ye brought that which was torn, and the lame, and the sick... should I accept this of your hand? saith the LORD,' Malachi 1:13, the dishonor of offering God less than the best, the New Testament fulfilment — the unblemished offering points to Christ, 'a lamb without blemish and without spot,' who alone is the acceptable offering, 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God,' Hebrews 9:14; 1 Peter 1:19, the figure — the perfection God demands, fulfilled in the spotless Lamb, and the call to offer God the best, not the blemished, in our worship and service); the blemished offering rejected (only the perfect accepted, Malachi 1:13) — the perfection (the blemished refused); the offering not acceptable if blemished (the perfection required for acceptance, the type of Christ, Hebrews 9:14) — the figure (only the spotless acceptable, the providence, the perfect required, the blemished cases following, vv.22-24) — Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The peace offering must be perfect to be accepted: 'and whosoever offereth a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD to accomplish his vow, or a freewill offering in beeves or sheep, it shall be perfect to be accepted; there shall be no blemish therein' — the peace offering for a vow or freewill, like the burnt offering, to be perfect and without blemish to be accepted (the to accomplish his vow, the vow performed, the it shall be perfect to be accepted, the perfection required, the no blemish therein, the unblemished, the figure, the perfection required in the peace offering as in the burnt offering, the offering must be perfect to be accepted, the type of the perfect Christ, our peace, 'he is our peace,' Ephesians 2:14, the spotless offering that makes our peace with God); the peace offering for the vow (the offering of fellowship) — the offering (the peace offering bound by the law); 'it shall be perfect to be accepted; there shall be no blemish therein' (the perfection required, no blemish, the offering accepted only if perfect, the figure, the perfect Christ our peace, Ephesians 2:14, the providence, the perfect required, the blemishes named, vv.22-24) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And whosoever offereth a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD to accomplish his vow, or a freewill offering in beeves or sheep, it shall be perfect to be accepted; there shall be no blemish therein

the perfection required in the peace offering as in the burnt offering
the type of the perfect Christ our peace (Ephesians 2:14)

schools.summary: The blemishes that disqualify the offering: 'blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a wen, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these unto the LORD, nor make an offering by fire of them upon the altar unto the LORD' — animals blind, broken (with a fracture), maimed, or with a wen, scurvy, or scab not to be offered (the blind, broken, maimed, the gross defects, the wen, scurvy, scabbed, the diseases and blemishes, the not offer these... nor make an offering by fire, the blemished refused from the altar, the figure, the various blemishes that disqualify, God will not be served with the defective, the perfection required, the rebuke of offering God the blind and the lame, Malachi 1:8, the spotless offering required, fulfilled in Christ); the gross defects (blind, broken, maimed) disqualifying the offering (Malachi 1:8) — the perfection (the defective refused); 'a wen, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these' (the diseases and blemishes disqualifying, the figure, the perfection required, the type of Christ, the providence, the blemished refused from the altar) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Gill.

themes: Blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a wen, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these unto the LORD, nor make an offering by fire of them upon the altar unto the LORD

schools.summary: The exception for the freewill offering: 'either a bullock or a lamb that hath any thing superfluous or lacking in his parts, that mayest thou offer for a freewill offering; but for a vow it shall not be accepted' — an animal with a part overgrown (superfluous) or stunted (lacking), a lesser defect, might be offered for a freewill offering but not for a vow (the superfluous or lacking in his parts, a minor disproportion, a lesser blemish, the offer for a freewill offering, permitted in the freewill, a lesser strictness, the but for a vow it shall not be accepted, the vow requiring the perfect, the figure, the distinction between the freewill and the vow, the vow, a debt promised, requiring the perfect, the freewill, a spontaneous gift, allowing the lesser, yet the gross blemishes still barred even from the freewill, vv.22, the perfection especially required where a vow binds); the minor disproportion allowed in the freewill (the lesser strictness) — the exception (the freewill offering); 'but for a vow it shall not be accepted' (the vow requiring the perfect, the binding promise needing the unblemished, the figure, the perfection especially required where a vow binds, the providence, the vow strict, the freewill allowing the lesser) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: Either a bullock or a lamb that hath any thing superfluous or lacking in his parts, that mayest thou offer for a freewill offering; but for a vow it shall not be accepted
the exception for the freewill offering
the minor defect allowed in the freewill but not the vow
the perfection especially required where a vow binds

schools.summary: The prohibition of the bruised and damaged offering: 'ye shall not offer unto the LORD that which is bruised, or crushed, or broken, or cut; neither shall ye make any offering thereof in your land' — animals damaged in their members (bruised, crushed, broken, or cut, especially as understood of emasculation) not to be offered, nor such practice done in the land (the bruised, crushed, broken, or cut, the damaged members, understood by many of castration, the not offer... nor make any offering thereof in your land, the prohibition extending to the practice, the figure, the perfection required, the damaged and mutilated refused, the wholeness demanded in the offering, the integrity of the creature as made by God, the offering must be whole and entire as the perfect Christ); the damaged or mutilated refused (the members bruised or cut) — the prohibition (the damaged offering refused); 'neither shall ye make any offering thereof in your land' (the prohibition extending even to the practice in the land, the figure, the wholeness demanded, the integrity of the creature, the providence, the damaged refused, the foreign offering forbidden, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: Ye shall not offer unto the LORD that which is bruised, or crushed, or broken, or cut; neither shall ye make any offering thereof in your land

schools.summary: The prohibition of the blemished offering even from a stranger: 'neither from a stranger's hand shall ye offer the bread of your God of any of these; because their corruption is in them, and blemishes be in them: they shall not be accepted for you' — even a blemished animal received from a stranger's hand not to be offered as the bread of God, for its corruption and blemish made it unacceptable (the stranger's hand, the offering received from a foreigner, the the bread of your God, the offerings, the food of the altar, the their corruption is in them, and blemishes, the defect making them unacceptable, the not be accepted, the rejection, the figure, no blemished offering acceptable, even from a stranger, the perfection required from every source, the offering must be without blemish whoever brings it, the corruption that bars from acceptance, the spotless offering of Christ alone acceptable, Hebrews 9:14); the blemished offering refused even from a stranger (the bread of God) — the prohibition (no source excepted); 'their corruption is in them, and blemishes be in them: they shall not be accepted' (the corruption and blemish barring acceptance, the figure, the perfection required, the spotless Christ alone acceptable, Hebrews 9:14, the providence, the blemished refused from every source) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Neither from a stranger's hand shall ye offer the bread of your God of any of these; because their corruption is in them, and blemishes be in them: they shall not be accepted for you

no blemished offering acceptable from any source, the perfection required
the spotless offering of Christ alone acceptable to God (Hebrews 9:14)

schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the offering's age and the dam and young: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the law of the offering's age (eight days old) and the kindness of not killing the dam and her young the same day (vv.27-28, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the law of the blemished offering, now the law of the age and the merciful provision concerning the dam and young, God giving these laws, showing his care reaching even into the manner and timing of the offering, and his tenderness even toward the creatures); the LORD speaking to Moses (the law of the offering's age) — the new section (the age and the mercy to creatures); the LORD spake unto Moses — the law of the age introduced (the providence, God's care in the manner of the offering) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the offering's age and the dam and young

his care reaching into the manner of the offering and his tenderness toward the creatures

schools.summary: The offering's age of eight days: 'when a bullock, or a sheep, or a goat, is brought forth, then it shall be seven days under the dam; and from the eighth day and thenceforth it shall be accepted for an offering made by fire unto the LORD' — a newborn animal to remain seven days with its mother, and only from the eighth day could be offered (the seven days under the dam, the young left with its mother the first week, the from the eighth day... accepted, the offering permitted from the eighth day, the figure, the tenderness that leaves the young with its mother, the natural order honored, the young not torn from the dam too soon, the eighth day as in circumcision, Leviticus 12:3, the offering not premature, the care of God even for the newborn creature, the kindness woven into the law); the young left seven days with its mother (the natural order honored) — the tenderness (the young with the dam); 'from the eighth day... it shall be accepted' (the offering from the eighth day, the young old enough, as in circumcision, Leviticus 12:3, the figure, the offering not premature, the care for the creature, the providence, the offering's age fixed, the mercy to the dam, vv.28) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: When a bullock, or a sheep, or a goat, is brought forth, then it shall be seven days under the dam; and from the eighth day and thenceforth it shall be accepted for an offering made by fire unto the LORD
the offering's age of eight days
the young left with its mother the first week, the natural order honored
the care of God even for the newborn creature (Leviticus 12:3)

schools.summary: The mercy of not killing the dam and young the same day: 'and whether it be cow or ewe, ye shall not kill it and her young both in one day' — the merciful law — 'ye shall not kill it and her young both in one day': the cow or ewe and her young not to be killed on the same day (the witnesses noting the significance — the tenderness of God reaching even to the animals, the dam and her young not to be slain together, the natural bond spared, a stroke against cruelty, the lesson — the mercy and gentleness God weaves into his law, the law that teaches kindness even to the beasts, the avoiding of needless cruelty, the same tenderness as the law forbidding to take the mother bird with the young, Deuteronomy 22:6-7, and to seethe a kid in its mother's milk, Exodus 23:19, the wider truth — 'a righteous man regardeth the life of his beast,' Proverbs 12:10, and Paul's word that even the law about the ox shows God's care reaches beyond the beast to teach men, 'doth God take care for oxen? or saith he it altogether for our sakes?' 1 Corinthians 9:9-10, the figure — the tenderness and mercy of God reaching even to the creatures, the law that fosters compassion and forbids cruelty, the gentleness becoming those who serve a merciful God); the cow or ewe and her young (the natural bond, Deuteronomy 22:6-7) — the mercy (the dam and young spared together); the not killing both the same day (the tenderness, the avoiding of cruelty, Proverbs 12:10) — the figure (the mercy reaching the creatures, 1 Corinthians 9:9-10, the providence, the gentleness woven into the law) — Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the law against killing the dam and young the same day teach?

sides.position: The tenderness of God reaching even to the animals, the natural bond spared, a stroke against cruelty (the same gentleness as the law of the mother bird, Deuteronomy 22:6-7) - the mercy that 'regardeth the life of his beast' (Proverbs 12:10) and teaches men compassion (1 Corinthians 9:9-10), the gentleness becoming those who serve a merciful God

themes: And whether it be cow or ewe, ye shall not kill it and her young both in one day

the mercy that regardeth the life of his beast and teaches men compassion (Proverbs 12:10; 1 Corinthians 9:9-10)

schools.summary: The thank offering offered freely: 'and when ye will offer a sacrifice of thanksgiving unto the LORD, offer it at your own will' — the thank offering (a kind of peace offering, given in thanksgiving) to be offered freely, for acceptance (the sacrifice of thanksgiving, the offering of praise and gratitude, the offer it at your own will, freely, for your acceptance, the figure, the thank offering of praise, the free offering of gratitude, the figure of the sacrifice of praise we offer, 'by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually,' Hebrews 13:15, the freewill thanksgiving of the grateful heart); the offering of thanksgiving (the praise offering) — the offering (the thank offering); 'offer it at your own will' (offered freely, for acceptance, the willing thanksgiving, the figure, the sacrifice of praise, Hebrews 13:15, the providence, the thank offering eaten the same day, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And when ye will offer a sacrifice of thanksgiving unto the LORD, offer it at your own will

the free offering of gratitude, the sacrifice of praise we offer (Hebrews 13:15)
the freewill thanksgiving of the grateful heart

schools.summary: The thank offering eaten the same day: 'on the same day it shall be eaten up; ye shall leave none of it until the morrow: I am the LORD' — the thank offering to be eaten the same day, none left until morning (the eaten up the same day, the offering consumed that day, as the thank offering, Leviticus 7:15, the leave none until the morrow, nothing kept over, the figure, the thank offering eaten the same day, the freshness of the thanksgiving, nothing kept to corrupt, the figure of the present, fresh gratitude, the thanksgiving offered and enjoyed without delay, the offering not left to spoil); the thank offering eaten the same day (the offering consumed, Leviticus 7:15) — the freshness (the thanksgiving present); 'leave none of it until the morrow: I am the LORD' (nothing left over, the offering not kept to corrupt, the figure, the present, fresh gratitude, the providence, the thank offering eaten fresh, the law sealed) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge to keep and do God's commandments: 'therefore shall ye keep my commandments, and do them: I am the LORD' — the people to keep God's commandments and do them, sealed by 'I am the LORD' (the keep my commandments, the obedience required, the and do them, not only knowing but doing, the I am the LORD, the covenant ground and seal, the figure, the obedience required, the keeping and doing of the commandments, the doing as well as the hearing, 'be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only,' James 1:22, the commandments grounded in the LORD, the obedience owed to the covenant God); the keeping and doing of the commandments (the obedience, the doing as well as the hearing, James 1:22) — the obedience (the doing of the law); 'I am the LORD' (the covenant ground and seal, the authority, the figure, the obedience grounded in the LORD, the providence, the commandments sealed by the divine name) — Ellicott, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge not to profane but to hallow God's name: 'neither shall ye profane my holy name; but I will be hallowed among the children of Israel: I am the LORD which hallow you' — the hallowing — 'neither shall ye profane my holy name; but I will be hallowed among the children of Israel: I am the LORD which hallow you': the people not to profane God's holy name but to hallow it, and God himself would be hallowed among them, the LORD who sanctifies them (the witnesses noting the significance — the negative and the positive, not to profane, but to hallow God's name, the lesson — the hallowing of God's name, God to be reverenced and sanctified among his people, his name hallowed by their obedience and worship, the very petition the Lord taught, 'Hallowed be thy name,' Matthew 6:9, the I will be hallowed among the children of Israel, God's resolve to be sanctified among them, whether by their obedience or, if they profane, by his judgments, 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me,' Leviticus 10:3, the I am the LORD which hallow you, God the sanctifier, who makes them holy and is hallowed in them, vv.9, 16, the figure — the hallowing of God's name as the end of all the law, the people called to sanctify God in their hearts and lives, and God who both hallows them and is hallowed among them, 'sanctify the Lord God in your hearts,' 1 Peter 3:15); the not profaning but hallowing of God's name (God sanctified among them, Matthew 6:9; Leviticus 10:3) — the hallowing (God reverenced among his people); God the sanctifier who hallows his people (the LORD who makes them holy, 1 Peter 3:15) — the figure (God who both hallows and is hallowed, the providence, the name hallowed, the people sanctified, vv.33) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'I will be hallowed among the children of Israel: I am the LORD which hallow you' teach?

sides.position: The hallowing of God's name (the very petition 'Hallowed be thy name,' Matthew 6:9), God's resolve to be sanctified among his people whether by their obedience or his judgments (Leviticus 10:3), and the LORD who himself sanctifies them (1 Peter 3:15) - God who both hallows them and is hallowed in them

schools.summary: The LORD who brought them out of Egypt to be their God: 'that brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: I am the LORD' — the ground of all the commands: the LORD who brought Israel out of Egypt to be their God (the brought you out of the land of Egypt, the redemption from bondage, the to be your God, the purpose of the redemption, the covenant relation, the I am the LORD, the seal, the figure, the redemption from Egypt the ground of the obedience and holiness required, the redeemed belonging to God, the truth that redemption is the ground of obedience, 'ye are not your own... ye are bought with a price,' 1 Corinthians 6:19-20, the God who redeems claims his people for himself, the holiness flowing from the redemption, closing the chapter and the section); the LORD who brought Israel out of Egypt (the redemption, the ground of obedience) — the redemption (from bondage); 'to be your God: I am the LORD' (the redemption that they might be his, the covenant relation, 1 Corinthians 6:19-20, the figure, the redeemed belonging to God, the holiness flowing from redemption, the providence, the LORD who redeemed, the chapter sealed) — Clarke, Gill.
